title: Herbal linen sachets
subtitle: Muslin sachets filled with lavender, rosemary, and orris root for drawers and wardrobes
excerpt: Dried lavender and rosemary both deter moths and refresh stored linen. Orris root powder fixes the fragrance and slows evaporation. The sachets are made from squares of muslin tied with ribbon: simple and refillable. Replace the filling every 12 months.
sourceNotes: Herbal sachet formulation from Mrs Beeton's Household Management (1861, reprint). Lavender moth deterrence from the Royal Entomological Society. Orris root as fixative from Arctander, Perfume and Flavor Materials of Natural Origin (1960).

recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Make a larger batch and give as gifts or place throughout the house.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make in advance and refresh the essential oil every 6 months when the natural fragrance fades. The dried herbs remain effective as a moth deterrent even when the fragrance is less noticeable.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 12 months at full fragrance intensity. The dried herbs last longer as a moth deterrent: lavender and rosemary retain their insecticidal properties after the noticeable fragrance fades. Add 3 to 4 drops of lavender essential oil to the sachet at 6 months to extend the life.

glossaryTerms.definition: The effect of certain plants and essential oils on clothes moths (Tineola bisselliella), which are deterred by the volatile oils of lavender, rosemary, cedarwood, and cloves. These are deterrents rather than killers: they discourage moths from laying eggs near the herbs. Effective when the filling is fresh and placed in direct contact with stored woolens and natural-fibre textiles.

content.text: agents. Lavender contains mainly linalool; rosemary contains borneol and camphor. These compounds interfere with clothes moth behaviour and stop them laying eggs near the herbs. Orris root powder is the traditional fixative. It binds the fragrance molecules and releases them slowly over months rather than weeks. A sachet made with fresh filling remains effective for a full year.

Place the sachets in direct contact with wool, cashmere, and natural-fibre textiles: the volatile oils need to be close to the fibres to be effective. A sachet placed in a drawer works better than one hanging in the middle of a large wardrobe. Replace the filling or add fresh essential oil annually, before the spring, when clothes moths become active.

items.prepNote: The main moth-deterrent herb; use fully dried Lavandula angustifolia buds
Reinforces the lavender and adds a sharp herbal note
Fragrance fixative; extends the effective life of the sachet
Optional; add to the dried herbs before filling to boost the initial fragrance
15 cm squares of pre-washed muslin or cheesecloth; one per sachet
20 cm lengths of ribbon or cotton twine for tying

content.text: Combine the dried lavender flowers, dried rosemary, and orris root powder in a bowl. If using the lavender essential oil, add it drop by drop to the dried herbs and stir through before mixing in the orris root powder. The herbs should feel slightly damper and more fragrant after adding the oil.

Lay a muslin square flat. Place approximately 25 g of filling in the centre. Gather the corners up and tie firmly with ribbon or twine. Repeat for each sachet. Place in drawers, wardrobes, or shoe boxes in contact with stored natural-fibre clothing.

12 months at full fragrance intensity. To refresh: untie the sachet, add 3 to 4 drops of lavender essential oil to the filling, and retie. The sachets can also be refilled with fresh herbs if the filling has fully dried out and lost its fragrance.
